title: Pleading
summary: Pleading sets the claims and defenses in motion and frames what must be plausibly alleged and timely challenged.
learningObjectives: Identify the minimum components of a complaint.
Apply the plausibility standard to factual allegations.
Track amendment timing and relation-back basics.

rules.title: Rule 8 Basics
rules.blackLetterLaw: A claim for relief generally requires a short and plain statement of the grounds for jurisdiction, the claim showing entitlement to relief, and a demand for judgment.
rules.explanation: Federal pleading is meant to be concise, but it must still give fair notice and enough factual matter to support relief.
elements: Short and plain statement of the claim
pitfalls: Reciting legal conclusions without supporting facts
Overlooking special pleading rules for fraud or mistake

rules.title: Plausibility and Rule 12(b)(6)
rules.blackLetterLaw: To survive a Rule 12(b)(6) motion, the complaint must contain enough factual matter, accepted as true, to state a plausible claim for relief.
rules.explanation: Courts separate factual allegations from conclusory assertions and ask whether the facts plausibly suggest liability.
elements: Ask whether the facts make relief plausible, not merely possible
pitfalls: Treating notice pleading as no-facts pleading

rules.title: Amendments
rules.blackLetterLaw: A party may amend once as a matter of course within the Rule 15 timing window; otherwise amendment requires consent or leave, which should be freely given when justice so requires.
rules.explanation: Courts usually permit amendments absent reasons like undue delay, bad faith, repeated failure, prejudice, or futility.
elements: One amendment as of right within the prescribed time
Later amendments by consent or leave of court
Relation back can preserve timeliness when the rule's requirements are met
pitfalls: Assuming any amendment automatically relates back
Ignoring prejudice to the opposing party

questions.prompt: A complaint alleges that the defendant 'acted unlawfully' but offers almost no supporting facts. The defendant moves to dismiss. Which is the best argument for dismissal?

choices.text: The complaint fails because it lacks a jury demand
The complaint is implausible because it relies on conclusory assertions
The complaint must be dismissed unless every element is proven
The complaint is invalid because it is short

questions.rationale: A pleading that offers labels or conclusions without enough factual matter is vulnerable under Rule 12(b)(6).

questions.prompt: When should leave to amend generally be granted after the as-of-right period expires?

choices.text: Never, because federal pleading is final once filed
Only if the opposing party cannot object
Freely when justice so requires, absent reasons like prejudice or futility
Only before any motion to dismiss is filed

questions.rationale: Rule 15 reflects a liberal amendment policy, limited by concerns such as prejudice, delay, or futility.
